text: Appropriate training load monitoring can aid in determining whether an athlete is adapting to a training program and in minimizing the risk of developing non-functional overreaching, illness, and/or injury.

Dissociation between external load (e.g. power output, distance) and internal load (e.g. heart rate, RPE) may reveal the state of fatigue of an athlete, serving as an early warning sign of maladaptation.

No single definitive marker of training load or fatigue has been identified; effective monitoring requires combining multiple markers including RPE, heart rate, blood lactate, and training impulse (TRIMP).

Heart rate recovery, neuromuscular function testing, biochemical/hormonal/immunological assessments, questionnaires, psychomotor speed, and sleep quality/quantity are all useful monitoring tools in high-performance programs.
Training load monitoring must be individualized to each athlete rather than applying universal thresholds, as individual responses to the same external load can vary significantly.

Effective monitoring systems should be intuitive, provide efficient data analysis and interpretation, and enable simple yet scientifically valid feedback to athletes and coaches to support practical decision-making.
